title: Authentication, Authorization, RBAC, Service Accounts, Tokens, and Impersonation · Guided Manifest Studio
summary: A platform pairing session predicts each API transition before producing the first working least-privilege identity and authorization policy for authentication, authorization, rbac, service accounts, tokens, and impersonation. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of least-privilege identity and authorization policy while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Trace TLS identity, authentication, user and group information, authorization, admission, validation, persistence, and audit for one request.
Separate external human users and groups, ServiceAccounts, node identities, workload identity federation, image pull identity, and bootstrap credentials.
Construct namespace-scoped Roles and RoleBindings or justified ClusterRoles and ClusterRoleBindings from verbs, resources, names, non-resource URLs, and subjects.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# kubernetes-basics cumulative orchestration portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never invoke kubectl, read kubeconfig, contact a cluster API, or execute host commands.
workspace: kubernetes
version-baseline:
  kubernetes: 1.36.2
  minikube: 1.38.1
  gateway-api: 1.5.1
  helm: 4.2.2
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-cluster
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop K8s Authn Authz Admission Chain
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop workload uses the default ServiceAccount, automounts a broad token, and receives wildcard cluster-admin through a group binding. Derive subject, verb, resource, namespace, token audience, impersonation, and least-privilege proof. Competency probe: Trace TLS identity, authentication, user and group information, authorization, admission, validation, persistence, and audit for one request.

What must be established before implementation?

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review
steps.instruction:
Append the Kubernetes evidence item headed "  # Evidence: k8s-token-audience-rotation-review-1xpxju". Tailor its rbac-policy.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic API or manifest review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run kubectl, Helm, kubeadm, and cluster commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns K8s Token Audience Rotation Review into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, keep K8s Token Audience Rotation Review connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: k8s-token-audience-rotation-review-1xpxju
  - competency: k8s-token-audience-rotation-review
    artifact: rbac-policy.yaml
    decision: token audience rotation review
    environment: kubeadm recovery cluster restored onto replacement nodes
    fault-injection: fill ephemeral storage before graceful termination
    retained-proof: Pod state previous exit endpoint readiness and request result
    case-key: 1xpxju
    change: |
      serviceAccount: intake/status-api
      automountServiceAccountToken: false
      token: projected-audience-intake-api
      role: get-configmap-status-only
      binding: namespace-intake
      review: auth-can-i-and-escalation-map
    verify: kubectl auth can-i --list --as system:serviceaccount:intake:status-api -n intake
    failure: Reject broad ClusterRoleBinding, long-lived static tokens, wildcard verbs, secret reads, bind or escalate paths, or workload creation as harmless.
    repair: Separate human and workload identity, scope verbs and resources, use bound audience tokens, test authorization, and review escalation periodically.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.

hints: For Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, name the context, namespace, API identity, desired state, observed status, controller, policy, and failure-domain boundary, K8s Token Audience Rotation Review precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, trace this concrete input and predict admission, defaulting, scheduling, reconciliation, status, traffic, storage, policy, disruption, and cleanup evidence before reviewing it: A workshop workload uses the default ServiceAccount, automounts a broad token, and receives wildcard cluster-admin through a group binding. Derive subject, verb, resource, namespace, token audience, impersonation, and least-privilege proof. Competency probe: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.

For Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, challenge the documented misconception with object, condition, event, policy, failure, repair, rollback, and transfer evidence: A projected service account token is valid forever and for every service that trusts the cluster issuer.

stimulus.title: K8s Token Audience Rotation Review failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A projected service account token is valid forever and for every service that trusts the cluster issuer.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated K8s Token Audience Rotation Review condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.

For test an acceptance boundary, copy one familiar manifest or kubectl command, omit context, API discovery, desired-versus-observed status, failure, rollback, and disposable-cluster transfer checks, then accept an Applied response as production proof.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for K8s Token Audience Rotation Review: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop workload uses the default ServiceAccount, automounts a broad token, and receives wildcard cluster-admin through a group binding. Derive subject, verb, resource, namespace, token audience, impersonation, and least-privilege proof. Competency probe: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.

Evidence target: Defend the transferred model · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide. For Defend the transferred model · Workshop K8s Token Audience Rotation Review, keep K8s Token Audience Rotation Review connected to mechanically reviewable Kubernetes object, status, and transfer evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use TokenRequest and projected bound tokens with audience, expiry, rotation, revocation, automount controls, authorization checks, and periodic review.
